summary: Dan Shapiro demonstrates Google AI Studio's Apps feature by giving it the ultimate test: pasting in XKCD 1425 (the famous comic about how identifying if a photo contains a bird was once a nearly impossible CS problem) with no additional instructions. The comic, published in 2014, joked that bird detection would take five years of research — a task now trivially solved by AI. Shapiro lets the results speak for themselves, implicitly making the point that AI tools have made previously impossible software tasks almost effortless. The brevity of the post is itself the argument: the feat requires so little effort it barely needs explanation.
key_points: Google AI Studio's Apps feature is capable enough to interpret and build from just an image/comic as input
XKCD 1425's 'impossible' bird-detection task is now trivially solvable, demonstrating how far AI has come
The post's extreme brevity (57 words) is a deliberate stylistic choice — the simplicity of the demo IS the point
AI prototyping tools have reached a level where you can build functional apps without writing any code or even any instructions
money_quotes: I decided to give it the ultimate challenge: I pasted in XKCD 1425, and nothing else.
I'll let you be the judge of the results.
key_insight: The fact that an AI tool can build a working app from nothing but a comic about an impossible programming task — with zero written instructions — is itself the most powerful argument for how far AI development tools have come.
